text.value: So, im having an issue with this code. Everything works as when its local, but when i deploy my backend apollo graphql server to heroku, the getCurrentUser query that i have doesn't work correctly. It returns data then it is set to null. I have no idea what it is. I'll call the login mutation in login.tsx, and then it should set the variables to the current user. I'm thinking maybe it's a session issue?

There could be a few reasons why getCurrentUser is returning null after initially returning data when deployed to Heroku:
1. Session expiration - Since Heroku dynos can restart/cycle periodically, it's possible the user's session is getting cleared, causing them to be logged out. You may need to implement some kind of session persistence like redis to maintain user sessions across dyno restarts.
2. Race conditions - If getCurrentUser is called before the login mutation finishes, it may temporarily return null before the user is fully logged in. You may need to handle async logic better to wait for login to fully complete.
3. Cache issues - Apollo Client caches query results, so if the cached value is null, it may keep returning that. You may need to update the cache after login to set the current user data.
4. Environment differences - If the Heroku and local environments differ, e.g. in databases, secret keys, etc, it could cause login to fail on Heroku. Double check your config across environments.
5. Logging - Add more logging or debugging to try and pinpoint where exactly it starts returning null. e.g. is it during login, on refetches, or only after some time?
So in summary, focus on session handling, async logic, caching, environmental differences and adding more logging to debug further. That should help uncover what is causing the user to get logged out on Heroku.

Here's the rest of my logs: login-430846e234e8c4aa.js:1 Current User before login mutation Object login-430846e234e8c4aa.js:1 Current User before login mutation, userHook Object _app-927d3b77fc93fd01.js:40 Current User in User Provider Object _app-927d3b77fc93fd01.js:40 Current User memo Object login-430846e234e8c4aa.js:1 Current User after login mutation and before refetch ObjectgetCurrentUser: null[[Prototype]]: Object login-430846e234e8c4aa.js:1 Current User after login mutation and before refetch with userHook ObjectgetCurrentUser: null[[Prototype]]: Object _app-927d3b77fc93fd01.js:40 Current User in User Provider ObjectgetCurrentUser: {__typename: 'User', id: '65f1511bb780a0113fe5d1b1', firstName: 'Taffy', email: 'taffy@gmail.com', entries: 25, …}[[Prototype]]: Object _app-927d3b77fc93fd01.js:40 Current User memo Objectemail: "taffy@gmail.com"entries: 25firstName: "Taffy"id: "65f1511bb780a0113fe5d1b1"isActive: trueisLoggedIn: true__typename: "User"[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in application page Objectemail: "taffy@gmail.com"entries: 25firstName: "Taffy"id: "65f1511bb780a0113fe5d1b1"isActive: trueisLoggedIn: true__typename: "User"[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in application page userHook Objectemail: "taffy@gmail.com"entries: 25firstName: "Taffy"id: "65f1511bb780a0113fe5d1b1"isActive: trueisLoggedIn: true__typename: "User"[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in AuthGuard ObjectgetCurrentUser: {__typename: 'User', id: '65f1511bb780a0113fe5d1b1', firstName: 'Taffy', email: 'taffy@gmail.com', entries: 25, …}[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in AuthGuard userHook Objectemail: "taffy@gmail.com"entries: 25firstName: "Taffy"id: "65f1511bb780a0113fe5d1b1"isActive: trueisLoggedIn: true__typename: "User"[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in application page Objectemail: "taffy@gmail.com"entries: 25firstName: "Taffy"id: "65f1511bb780a0113fe5d1b1"isActive: trueisLoggedIn: true__typename: "User"[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in application page userHook Objectemail: "taffy@gmail.com"entries: 25firstName: "Taffy"id: "65f1511bb780a0113fe5d1b1"isActive: trueisLoggedIn: true__typename: "User"[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in AuthGuard ObjectgetCurrentUser: {__typename: 'User', id: '65f1511bb780a0113fe5d1b1', firstName: 'Taffy', email: 'taffy@gmail.com', entries: 25, …}[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in AuthGuard userHook Objectemail: "taffy@gmail.com"entries: 25firstName: "Taffy"id: "65f1511bb780a0113fe5d1b1"isActive: trueisLoggedIn: true__typename: "User"[[Prototype]]: Object _app-927d3b77fc93fd01.js:40 Current User in User Provider ObjectgetCurrentUser: null[[Prototype]]: Object _app-927d3b77fc93fd01.js:40 Current User memo Object[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in application page Objectemail: "taffy@gmail.com"entries: 25firstName: "Taffy"id: "65f1511bb780a0113fe5d1b1"isActive: trueisLoggedIn: true__typename: "User"[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in application page userHook Object[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in AuthGuard ObjectgetCurrentUser: {__typename: 'User', id: '65f1511bb780a0113fe5d1b1', firstName: 'Taffy', email: 'taffy@gmail.com', entries: 25, …}[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in AuthGuard userHook Object[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in application page Object[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in application page userHook Object[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in AuthGuard ObjectgetCurrentUser: {__typename: 'User', id: '65f1511bb780a0113fe5d1b1', firstName: 'Taffy', email: 'taffy@gmail.com', entries: 25, …}[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in AuthGuard userHook Object[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in AuthGuard ObjectgetCurrentUser: null[[Prototype]]: Object application-3da580b74574444c.js:1 User in AuthGuard userHook Object[[Prototype]]: Object _app-927d3b77fc93fd01.js:40 Current User in User Provider ObjectgetCurrentUser: null[[Prototype]]: Object _app-927d3b77fc93fd01.js:40 Current User memo Object[[Prototype]]: Object

Based on the additional logs you provided, it seems the issue is likely related to session handling/expiration.
The key things I notice:
- The user object is initially populated after login - It persists for a bit across component re-renders - But later on, getCurrentUser starts returning null
This points to the user's session being cleared or expiring, causing them to get logged out.
Some things you could try:
- Implement session persistence using something like redis to maintain sessions across Heroku dyno restarts/cycles.
- Reduce the session expiration time so sessions don't last as long.
- Refresh the user's auth token periodically using something like a setInterval to keep the session alive.
- When getCurrentUser returns null, force log the user out completely to avoid partial logged in state.
- Double check your session and authentication related middleware, cookies, and headers are properly configured.
The core issue is that Heroku can't maintain state for long periods, so you need to implement sticky sessions. Using redis or periodically refreshing the auth should help avoid the session getting cleared.

Here are a few things you could try to improve session handling in your Express server:
1. Use express-session's resave and saveUninitialized options more wisely:
```js app.use( session({ //... resave: false, saveUninitialized: true }) ); ```
- `resave: false` avoids re-saving the session on every request which is unnecessary.
- `saveUninitialized: true` saves a session even if it is "uninitialized" which is better for login flows.
2. Set the session cookie to be sameSite: 'none' and secure: true for production:
```js cookie: { //... sameSite: 'none', secure: true } ```
This enables the cookie to be sent on cross-site requests and forces HTTPS.
3. Use connect-redis to store sessions in a Redis database instead of the default in-memory store. This will persist sessions across server restarts on Heroku.
4. Implement refresh token logic to generate a new access token when the session expires, allowing seamless re-authentication.
5. Make sure CORS and credentials are properly configured on both client and server.
6. Verify sessions are being saved, cookies are set properly, and session middleware is running early enough.
7. Consider shorter session expiration times for security, and refresh tokens to re-authenticate.
Focus on connect-redis and refresh tokens as the main solutions. The other tips help cover all bases around secure session handling.

Based on the additional code you provided, here are a few things you could try:
1. Switch from MongoStore to connect-redis for session storage. MongoStore stores sessions in MongoDB which won't persist across Heroku dynos. Redis will store sessions externally and maintain them across restarts.
2. Implement refresh tokens - generate a refresh token on login along with the main JWT. Store refresh tokens in the database. When the JWT expires, use the refresh token to generate a new JWT. This avoids having to fully re-login when the session expires.
3. On the frontend, handle the case where getCurrentUser returns null by logging the user out completely and redirecting to the login page. Avoid partial logged in states.
4. Make sure to configure CORS properly on both frontend and backend. Set credentials: 'include' and origin to your frontend URL.
5. Try reducing the express session cookie maxAge to be shorter, like 1 hour instead of 24 hours. Shorter expiration times can avoid stale sessions.
6. Ensure session middleware is at the very top before other middleware and routes.
Overall, the key changes would be to switch to Redis for session storage, implement refresh tokens, handle null users by fully logging out, and double check CORS configuration. That should significantly improve session handling on the backend. Let me know if any of those need more clarification!
Could that explain why it's not working while its hosted? Could you write that code for me?

Based on your server code, a few things stand out that could be contributing to the session handling issues:
1. You are using MongoStore for session storage. This stores sessions in MongoDB rather than an external store like Redis. So sessions won't persist across Heroku dyno restarts.
2. No refresh token logic is implemented. So when the JWT expires after 24 hours, the user would have to fully re-login rather than seamlessly getting a new access token.
3. The context function always creates a new User model instance per request. Ideally this should be cached/memoized to avoid unnecessary overhead.
4. There is no handling of the case when getCurrentUser returns null (user logged out). The frontend likely needs to handle this by logging the user out completely.
5. CORS and credentials not configured in Apollo Server. Need to enable CORS and credentials to allow the frontend to send cookies.
To summarize, the two main issues are:
1. Sessions are not persisted externally in something like Redis, so are lost when Heroku dynos restart.
2. No refresh token logic to smoothly get a new JWT if the main token expires.
To fix this, I'd recommend:
1. Switching to connect-redis for session storage.
2. Implementing refresh tokens to avoid full re-login when JWT expires.
3. Memoizing the User model in context for performance.
4. Handling null users on frontend by logging out completely.
5. Configuring CORS credentials and origin in Apollo Server.
Let me know if any part of the analysis or recommendations need more explanation!

Based on the client code you shared, a couple things stand out:
- The credentials option is good to send cookies with requests.
- However, the CORS configuration may still be missing on the server side.
Specifically, the Apollo Server needs to allow credentials and whitelist your frontend URL in the CORS options, like:
```js const server = new ApolloServer({ // ... cors: { origin: 'https://my-frontend-url.com', credentials: true } }) ```
Without properly configured CORS, the browser may still block sending cookies to the GraphQL server.
Additionally, on the frontend, you may need to set the credentials mode in the fetch call when logging in:
```js fetch('/login', { credentials: 'include' }) ```
This ensures cookies are sent with the initial request that sets the session.
So in summary, make sure CORS origins and credentials are enabled on both the Apollo Server and in fetch requests on the frontend.
The other thing to check would be that the session middleware is configured before other app routes in Express.
But the main issue is likely just CORS configuration not allowing the browser to send cookies cross-origin properly. Let me know if that helps explain where the problem could be!

The CORS configuration in your Express app looks good! You have the credentials and origin whitelisting in place.
One other thing to check is that the `credentials: 'include'` option is set when making requests from the frontend:
```js // Example fetch request fetch('/graphql', { credentials: 'include' }) ```
This ensures that cookies are properly sent in the initial request to set the session.
You mentioned that making the login mutation and then getCurrentUser query works when done sequentially.
In that case, my suspicion is that the cookies/credentials are not being sent properly in the initial login request from the frontend.
So I would try:
1. Adding `credentials: 'include'` to the fetch request on login.
2. Logging the request headers on the backend to verify cookies are sent.
3. Checking if subsequent requests properly receive the session cookie.
That should help confirm if the credentials/cookies are being sent and received as expected.
Let me know if you have any other questions!

yes it works with postman but not with in my localhosted code. Look at @.../frontend/contexts/UserContext.tsx @.../frontend/components/AuthGaurd.tsx @.../backend/server/app.ts @.../backend/server/server.ts to find the problem

Based on reviewing the UserContext, AuthGuard, app.ts and server.ts files, a few things stand out that could be causing the issue with sessions not being maintained from the frontend code:
1. In UserContext.tsx, the GET_CURRENT_USER query is being made on every render. This will cause a lot of unnecessary requests. It would be better to memoize the data and only refetch when needed.
2. There is no error handling if getCurrentUser returns null. As mentioned earlier, this should log the user out completely.
3. In AuthGuard.tsx, again GET_CURRENT_USER is being called on every render. Should be memoized.
4. No check for loading state in AuthGuard. Need to handle case when data is undefined.
5. In app.ts, double check session middleware is above other app routes.
6. Enable CORS credentials in Apollo Server in server.ts.
To summarize, the main fixes would be:
- Memoize GET_CURRENT_USER data in context and AuthGuard
- Handle null user by logging out completely in UserContext
- Check for loading state in AuthGuard
- Enable CORS credentials in Apollo Server
- Ensure session middleware comes before routes
- Make sure credentials: 'include' is set in frontend requests
This will minimize unnecessary requests, handle invalid users properly, and ensure credentials are enabled end-to-end.
Let me know if any of those need more explanation or if you have any other questions!
I'm not worried about performance at this time, i want to focus on functionality. I just want to know why im getting null back when my server is hosted

Look at @.../frontend/pages/login.tsx @.../frontend/contexts/UserContext.tsx @.../frontend/components/AuthGaurd.tsx @.../backend/server/app.ts @.../backend/server/server.ts @.../frontend/config/apolloClient.ts @.../graphql/resolvers/UserResolvers.ts
Remember, im hosting with heroku, im able to preform the correct operations in postman, but with my frontend code, it's not working

Taking another look at the files you mentioned, here are a few things that stand out:
In login.tsx:
- When making the login request, make sure to set `credentials: 'include'` to send cookies
- After a successful login, force a refresh of the user data in the context by calling `getUserData()`
In UserContext.tsx:
- Memoize the user data to prevent unnecessary re-fetches
- Log out user completely if data is undefined
In AuthGuard.tsx:
- Check for loading state before checking user
- Redirect on failure instead of returning null
In app.ts:
- Double check session middleware is before routes
In server.ts:
- Enable CORS credentials in Apollo Server
- Memoize User model in context
In apolloClient.ts:
- Ensure URI is correct when hosted
In UserResolvers.ts:
- On login, call `req.session.save()` to persist session
The main issues seem to be:
- Credentials not being sent on login
- Session not being persisted on login
- Loading state not handled
- URI incorrect when hosted
Focus on those areas - sending credentials properly, saving session on login, loading states, and URI. That should help resolve the problems with credentials and sessions not being maintained when hosted. Let me know if any of those need more explanation!
Show me these changes with the actual code.
This code isn't correct for my project. Im using Next.ts. Did you even look at the files?

look at @.../backend/server/server.ts and @.../backend/server/app.ts again to see if something is missing for hosted production. This app also works fine locally in production, just not with a hosted server endpoint
Well im not using the default memory store, im using MongoStore
my localhost is using http, will this cause issues with the cookie settings?

Good catch! Using HTTP instead of HTTPS for your local development environment can definitely cause issues with the cookie settings when deploying to production.
A few things to keep in mind:
- In development, the session cookie won't be flagged as secure since the requests are over HTTP.
- But in production, when you enable `secure: true` on the cookie, the browser will reject sending the cookie over HTTP.
- So you'll want to allow an insecure cookie in dev, but secure in prod:
```js session({ cookie: { secure: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production' } }) ```
- Similarly, you'll want to allow 'sameSite: none' in production for cross-origin cookies, but can relax the restriction in development.
- For local dev, you'll need to allow credentials and origins from your frontend URL in CORS.
- In production, lock down the CORS origins to only your frontend domain.
So in summary:
- Cookie secure only in production
- SameSite none only in production
- Allow frontend CORS in dev
- Lock down CORS in prod
This will allow your local HTTP environment to work, while still securing everything properly in production.
Let me know if that helps explain how to handle the HTTP vs HTTPS differences between dev and prod!
im not getting any cors errors within @.../backend/server/server.ts

If you are not getting any CORS errors in your server.ts file, then your CORS configuration is likely not the issue preventing sessions from working properly in production.
A few other things I would check:
- In your frontend code, ensure you are setting credentials: 'include' on requests, especially the initial login request. This sends the cookies.
- Verify the session cookie is actually being sent with requests from the frontend by checking the Network tab in devtools.
- Print out req.session in the context function in server.ts to validate a session is being created.
- Make sure session middleware comes before any routes that depend on it.
- Call req.session.save() after creating a new session to persist it.
- Handle cases where user data comes back null in frontend by logging out.
- Check for Apollo Server middleware being applied before routes.
So the focus would be on:
- Sending credentials properly from frontend
- Verifying session cookie is sent
- Logging session server-side to debug
- Session order in Express
- Saving new sessions
- Null user handling
- Apollo middleware order
Since CORS errors aren't happening, the issue likely lies in one of those areas related to credentials, cookies, session handling and timing.
Let me know if you need any help debugging or implementing changes related to those items!
With the login mutation, the cookie is being sent, but i dont see any cookies with the getCurrentUser query. Does the cookie have to be sent there as well or is it referenced in the login mutation?

Good observation - the session cookie should be sent automatically with all requests after the initial login, so it is expected behavior that the cookie wouldn't be present on the getCurrentUser query made before logging in.
Here is the typical cookie flow:
1. Login request sends credentials and sets session cookie
2. Session cookie is automatically sent in header for subsequent requests
3. Server checks for and validates session cookie on each request
4. User data returned if session is valid
So to summarize:
- The login request needs to send credentials to set the session cookie initially
- After that, the browser should automatically handle sending the cookie
- The server checks the cookie header on each request to validate the session
- No need to manually send credentials after the initial login
Some things you can try to debug:
- Check the session cookie is set after login by inspecting response headers
- Print out req.session in server context to validate session creation
- Make sure session middleware comes before route handlers
But in general - the flow of the cookie being set on login and automatically sent after that is expected and correct. Let me know if any part of the cookie flow needs more clarification!

Im trying to deploy my frontend to vercel and i get this error: RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded at RegExp.exec (<anonymous>) at create (/vercel/path0/frontend/node_modules/.pnpm/next@14.0.4_@babel+core@7.24.0_react-dom@18.2.0_react@18.2.0_sass@1.70.0/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/micromatch/index.js:15:18889) at create (/vercel/path0/frontend/node_modules/.pnpm/next@14.0.4_@babel+core@7.24.0_react-dom@18.2.0_react@18.2.0_sass@1.70.0/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/micromatch/index.js:15:18918) at parse.fastpaths (/vercel/path0/frontend/node_modules/.pnpm/next@14.0.4_@babel+core@7.24.0_react-dom@18.2.0_react@18.2.0_sass@1.70.0/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/micromatch/index.js:15:18997) at picomatch.makeRe (/vercel/path0/frontend/node_modules/.pnpm/next@14.0.4_@babel+core@7.24.0_react-dom@18.2.0_react@18.2.0_sass@1.70.0/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/micromatch/index.js:15:21635) at picomatch (/vercel/path0/frontend/node_modules/.pnpm/next@14.0.4_@babel+core@7.24.0_react-dom@18.2.0_react@18.2.0_sass@1.70.0/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/micromatch/index.js:15:19637) at /vercel/path0/frontend/node_modules/.pnpm/next@14.0.4_@babel+core@7.24.0_react-dom@18.2.0_react@18.2.0_sass@1.70.0/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/micromatch/index.js:15:19294 at Array.map (<anonymous>) at picomatch (/vercel/path0/frontend/node_modules/.pnpm/next@14.0.4_@babel+core@7.24.0_react-dom@18.2.0_react@18.2.0_sass@1.70.0/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/micromatch/index.js:15:19286) at micromatch.isMatch (/vercel/path0/frontend/node_modules/.pnpm/next@14.0.4_@babel+core@7.24.0_react-dom@18.2.0_react@18.2.0_sass@1.70.0/node_modules/next/dist/compiled/micromatch/index.js:15:1090) ELIFECYCLE Command failed with exit code 1. Error: Command "pnpm run build" exited with 1

This error with a maximum call stack size exceeded typically indicates some kind of infinite recursion in the code.
A few things to try:
- Look for any recursive component rendering in your React code that could cause an infinite loop. For example, a component that calls itself or a circular component dependency.
- Check for any recursive utility functions or hook callbacks that could be causing an infinite loop.
- The micromatch library mentioned in the stack trace is used for path matching. See if you have any custom code that uses micromatch and could be causing an infinite recursion.
- Try simplifying your app code and dependencies to isolate the issue - remove custom hooks, utils, etc one by one until the build succeeds.
- Upgrade all dependencies to latest versions in case this is a bug that has since been fixed.
- As a last resort, you may need to eject from Next.js and configure Babel to increase the call stack size limit.
Some things to focus on:
- Recursive component rendering - Custom hooks/utils with recursion - micromatch usage - Simplifying dependencies - Upgrading versions - Increasing call stack size
Let me know if any of those suggestions help uncover the source of the infinite recursion! The key is finding the one piece of code causing it and simplifying until the build passes.
